context: I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.
point_summary: John's greatest joy was not the absence of false teachers but that his children walked in truth, a joy realized when believers receive instruction and heed warning rather than surrendering to error.

context: look at what the Bible says here in 3 John, only one chapter here, but begin looking at verse number five.
point_summary: Gaius is held up as a concrete example of love toward all believers—including strangers—whose charity was publicly witnessed before the church.

context: I have no greater joy than to know that my children walk in truth.
point_summary: Passing faith to one's children and grandchildren is framed as the Christian's highest joy, drawn directly from John's declaration that he has no greater joy than to know his children walk in truth.

context: The book of 3rd John, the book of 3rd John all the way in the back of your Bible, 3rd John, Jude and Revelation, and just a very short letter, just 14 verses.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: As we think about the first eight verses to the short epistle of the book of 3 John,
point_summary: Third John preserves a brief but instructive portrait of an otherwise obscure believer, illustrating that God's recorded interactions with lesser-known figures still carry formative testimony for the church.

context: whom I love in the truth.
point_summary: John's address to Gaius as "well-beloved" and "whom I love in the truth" provides the sermon's title and introduces a man whose spiritual thriving stands in contrast to his apparent physical suffering.

context: Beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, even as thy soul prospereth.
point_summary: John's wish that Gaius prosper physically "even as thy soul prospereth" implies Gaius's spiritual health was flourishing even while his body apparently suffered illness.

context: For I rejoiced greatly when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee, even as thou walkest in the truth.
point_summary: Gaius's spiritual vitality—evidenced by brethren testifying of the truth in him and his walk in truth—demonstrates that one can thrive spiritually even amid physical weakness.

context: I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.
point_summary: No greater joy exists for a spiritual father than hearing that his children walk in truth, framing faithful obedience as the highest source of pastoral rejoicing.

context: Beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren and to strangers,
point_summary: Faithful service to fellow believers and strangers alike, as commended in verse 5, marks Gaius as one who was thriving spiritually even amid apparent physical weakness.

context: which have borne witness of thy charity before the church, whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort, thou shalt do well.
point_summary: Gaius's faithful service to traveling brethren, testified before the church, is presented as evidence of a spiritually thriving life even amid possible physical weakness.

context: Because that for his name's sake, they went forth taking nothing of the Gentiles,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we therefore ought to receive such that we might be fellow helpers to the truth.
point_summary: Supporting traveling ministers who take nothing from unbelievers creates a partnership in gospel truth, making such hospitality a shared labor for the sake of Christ's name.

context: I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.
point_summary: Verse 4's declaration of "no greater joy" applies most naturally to the spiritual mentoring relationship between John and Gaius, not biological parenthood, though its principle extends meaningfully to parenting.

context: I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.
point_summary: Though 3 John 4 is commonly applied to parenting, the sermon argues its primary meaning is spiritual: John's greatest joy was hearing that those he had discipled, like Gaius, were walking in truth and obedience to God's Word.

context: beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren and to strangers
point_summary: Gaius's faithful service to both brethren and strangers, summarized in verse 5, is presented as the defining mark of a servant whose obedience to God's word flowed from genuine love for God.

context: I believe that as we look at the life of Gaius and we note as we're looking at his testimony here in third John, that yes, he was a man who was obedient to God's word.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: ...how that he was walking in truth in verse number three...
point_summary: Gaius's "walking in truth" (v. 3) is presented as evidence of personal involvement in God's work, confirmed by his faithful service to both brethren and strangers.

context: ...how that in verse number five, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren and to the strangers.
point_summary: Gaius's faithful service to both brethren and strangers (v. 5) is presented as evidence of personal, active involvement in the work of God.

context: The Bible says that he loveth to have the preeminence.
point_summary: Diotrephes, who loved to have the preeminence, is cited as a biblical illustration of the pride-driven ambition Jesus directly contrasts with servant greatness.

context: Gaius, his soul prospereth
point_summary: Unconfessed sin blocks the true prosperity God desires — not material wealth, but the soul-flourishing John describes in wishing Gaius physical health equal to his spiritual health.

context: beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, even as my soul prospereth
point_summary: Covering sin forfeits the true prosperity God desires — the soul-prosperity of 3 John 1:2 — which exposes how little our physical comforts reflect genuine divine blessing.

context: I pray that you would be in good health that you would prosper and be in health Even as my soul prosperous
point_summary: John's prayer that Gaius prosper physically "even as" his soul prospered frames bodily and spiritual flourishing as parallel goods, with the soul's prosperity serving as the measure and model for outward blessing.

context: I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth
point_summary: Gaius exemplifies the "recommended and reputable" servant, whose greatest joy was hearing that those under his care walked in truth—confirming the character marks already drawn from 2 Corinthians 8.

context: thou Doest faithfully
point_summary: Gaius's faithfulness to "the brethren and to strangers" illustrates that genuine Christian service extends even to tasks and people beyond personal preference, done as unto the Lord.

context: Whatsoever thou doest to the brethren and to strangers I love verse number 5
point_summary: Faithful service to both brethren and strangers — regardless of personal preference — marks the kind of trustworthy character that earns a reputation for walking in truth.

context: thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest To the brethren and to strangers which have borne witness of thy charity before the church
point_summary: Faithful service to both familiar brethren and strangers marks Gaius as one who does "whatsoever" is assigned him diligently, not merely what he prefers.

context: Whom if thou bring forward on their journey after a godly sort thou shalt do well
point_summary: Faithful service to strangers, confirmed by the church's testimony, illustrates the "reputable" quality of a minister commended for walking in truth.

context: Because that for his namesake they went forth taking nothing of the Gentiles
point_summary: Going forth for the sake of the Name while taking nothing from the Gentiles marks these workers as wholly dependent on the support of fellow believers, making cooperation with them an obligation of truth.

context: verse number 8 that we might be fellow helpers to the truth
point_summary: Receiving and supporting those who go forth for the Name's sake makes believers "fellow helpers to the truth," a cooperative commitment that mirrors the partnership language Paul applies to Titus in 2 Corinthians 8.

context: even as thy soul prospereth
point_summary: God's desire for believers to prosper, drawn from 3 John 1:2's address to Gaius, is framed here as inward, soul-level flourishing rather than material success defined by bank accounts or possessions.

context: Gaius and 3 John
point_summary: Gaius, named in 3 John, illustrates that faithful servants of God need not hold prominent, household-name status to be profitable for the ministry as a clean vessel God uses.
